clean_story: Quantum fuzziness.  Clarity lost to noise. "Decoherence" describes how quantum weirdness—like particles being in two places at once—vanishes when they bump into the messy, jostling world around them. It’s like a spinning coin. While airborne, it’s both heads and tails. But the moment it hits the table—thunk—it picks one. That “table” is anything warm, big, or chaotic:a stray air molecule, a photon, even the hum of empty space. Here’s the catch:Decoherence isn’t destruction. The quantum info leaks into the environment, like ink bleeding into water. You can’t read the original message—but it’s still there, smeared across trillions of interactions. Example:Schrödinger’s cat (alive and dead) would decohere the instant a single light particle bounced off its fur. No observer needed—just the universe being its clumsy self. Lab tests show even vacuums cause decoherence. A lone electron’s quantum state collapses in milliseconds when exposed to the faintest electromagnetic whisper. Three related subjects are quantum entanglement, wavefunction collapse, and quantum error correction.
